primary-secondary.title: Primary vs. Secondary
primary-secondary.instructions: For each source, decide whether it is primary authority (the law itself) or a secondary source (something that explains or analyzes the law). After you answer, read the feedback — the reasoning is the point, not the score.

items.prompt: A section of the Arizona Revised Statutes governing a landlord’s duties.

feedback.correct: Right. A statute is the law itself, enacted by the legislature — a core primary authority.
feedback.secondary: A statute is not a commentary on the law; it is the law. Enacted text from a legislature is primary authority.

items.prompt: A United States Supreme Court opinion.

feedback.correct: Right. A judicial opinion is primary authority — the court is making and applying law, not summarizing it.
feedback.secondary: Cases are primary authority. The court is deciding the law, not describing someone else’s account of it.

items.prompt: A regulation published in the Code of Federal Regulations.

feedback.correct: Right. A validly issued regulation carries the force of law, so it is primary authority — even though it sits below the statute that authorized it.
feedback.secondary: Regulations are primary authority: properly adopted, they bind like law. Ranking below a statute does not make a source secondary.

items.prompt: A law review article analyzing a recent line of cases.

feedback.correct: Right. A law review article comments on and analyzes the law. It is secondary — a tool for understanding and finding primary authority.
feedback.primary: An article is not the law; it is one scholar’s analysis of it. That makes it a secondary source, however persuasive its argument.

items.prompt: A legal encyclopedia entry summarizing an area of law.

feedback.correct: Right. An encyclopedia describes the legal landscape and points you toward primary authority. It is a classic secondary source — a good place to start.
feedback.primary: An encyclopedia summarizes the law for orientation; it is not itself the law. That is the definition of a secondary source.

items.prompt: A section of the Restatement (Second) of Torts.

feedback.correct: Right — and this one trips people up. A Restatement reads like a code and is highly influential, but it is written by scholars, not enacted by a legislature or decided by a court. It is secondary, and never binding on its own.
feedback.primary: It looks like a statute, but a Restatement is drafted by the American Law Institute, not enacted into law. Influence is not the same as binding force — it is secondary.

items.prompt: The Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution.

feedback.correct: Right. A constitution is the supreme primary authority in its jurisdiction — nothing ranks higher.
feedback.secondary: A constitution is the foundational law itself, not a commentary on it. It is primary — and the highest primary authority there is.

items.prompt: A practitioner treatise on contract law (for example, a multi-volume set with annual supplements).

feedback.correct: Right. A treatise organizes and explains a doctrine and points you to the cases and statutes that govern it. Useful and respected — but secondary.
feedback.primary: A treatise is an expert’s synthesis of the law, not the law. It is a secondary source — the map, not the road.

binding-persuasive.title: Binding vs. Persuasive
binding-persuasive.instructions: Each item names the deciding court and an authority before it. Decide whether the court must follow the authority (binding) or may merely consider it (persuasive). Watch two things: the jurisdiction line and the court system.

items.prompt: Before the Arizona Court of Appeals: a prior Arizona Supreme Court decision on the same issue.

feedback.correct: Right. The Arizona Supreme Court is a higher court in the same system, so its decisions bind the Arizona Court of Appeals.
feedback.persuasive: Both courts sit in Arizona’s system, and the Supreme Court is above the Court of Appeals. A higher court in the same system binds — this is mandatory authority.

items.prompt: Before an Arizona trial court: a California Supreme Court decision squarely on point.

feedback.correct: Right. California’s law has no binding effect in Arizona. The decision may persuade, but the Arizona court is free to disregard it.
feedback.binding: Watch the jurisdiction line. A California decision — however on point — does not bind an Arizona court. It is persuasive only.

items.prompt: Before a U.S. District Court: a published decision of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the circuit in which that district sits.

feedback.correct: Right. The circuit court is the district court’s direct superior in the same federal system, so its precedent binds.
feedback.persuasive: The district court sits within that circuit, and the circuit court is above it in the same system. Its published precedent is binding.

items.prompt: Before the Arizona Supreme Court: a decision of the Arizona Court of Appeals (a lower court).

feedback.correct: Right. Precedent binds downward, not upward. A lower court’s decision does not bind the higher court — the Arizona Supreme Court may follow it or not.
feedback.binding: Binding effect runs from higher courts to lower courts in the same system, never the other way. A lower court cannot bind the court above it.

items.prompt: Before any state trial court: a U.S. Supreme Court decision interpreting the U.S. Constitution.

feedback.correct: Right. On the meaning of the U.S. Constitution, the U.S. Supreme Court binds every court in the country — state and federal alike.
feedback.persuasive: Federal constitutional rulings from the U.S. Supreme Court bind all courts. This is mandatory authority everywhere.

items.prompt: Before an Arizona trial court: a well-regarded treatise that states the rule clearly.

feedback.correct: Right. A treatise is a secondary source, and secondary sources are never binding — no matter how authoritative they sound.
feedback.binding: No secondary source binds a court. A treatise can persuade and can point you to binding authority, but it cannot control the outcome.

items.prompt: Before a U.S. District Court sitting in the Ninth Circuit: a decision of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit.

feedback.correct: Right. The Second Circuit is a coordinate court in a different part of the federal system; it does not bind a district court in the Ninth Circuit. Persuasive only.
feedback.binding: Only the district court’s own circuit binds it. A sister circuit’s decision is persuasive — useful, but the court need not follow it.

statute-common.title: Statute vs. Common Law
statute-common.instructions: Statutes and judge-made (common-law) rules often address the same ground. These items drill the rule from Chapter 1: a statute controls over common law on the same subject — and its limits.

items.prompt: For decades a state’s courts applied a common-law rule on a question. The legislature then enacted a statute squarely addressing the same question and setting a different rule. Which governs going forward?

options.text: Whichever a court prefers, case by case

feedback.correct: Right. On a subject a statute actually addresses, the statute controls over inconsistent common law. The legislature can displace or modify judge-made rules.
feedback.common: Common law yields when the legislature speaks to the same subject. The newer statute displaces the inconsistent common-law rule.
feedback.court: Courts do not pick freely between them. Where a statute squarely governs the subject, the court must apply the statute over conflicting common law.

items.prompt: On a particular question, the legislature has never enacted anything at all — no statute speaks to it. Where does the governing rule come from?

options.text: A statute you simply haven’t found yet
Nowhere — there is no law on the point

feedback.correct: Right. Where the legislature has not acted, the common law supplies the rule. Statutes displace common law only on subjects they actually address.
feedback.statute: Don’t assume a hidden statute. When the legislature truly has not spoken, the common law governs the question — that is its proper domain.
feedback.none: Silence by the legislature does not mean a legal vacuum. Courts supply the rule through the common law where no statute governs.

items.prompt: A statute governs the subject, but a word in it is ambiguous. A court issues an opinion interpreting that word. Does the court’s opinion now outrank the statute?

options.text: No — the statute still controls; the case supplies its meaning
Yes — the later case beats the earlier statute
Only if the court says it is overruling the statute

feedback.correct: Right. Interpreting a statute is not the same as overriding it. The statute remains the controlling law; the case settles what its words mean and how they apply.
feedback.yes: A court interpreting a statute does not displace it — the two work together. The statute controls; the opinion fixes its meaning. (And the legislature can amend the statute if it dislikes the reading.)
feedback.depends: A court cannot ‘overrule’ a statute by interpreting it. It clarifies the statute’s meaning; the statute itself remains the governing authority.

items.prompt: A statute is clear, but a litigant argues it violates the constitution. The court agrees. Which authority wins?

options.text: The constitution — the court can refuse to enforce the statute
The statute — enacted law always controls
The common law fills the gap automatically

feedback.correct: Right. The constitution sits above ordinary statutes. A court can hold a statute unconstitutional and refuse to enforce it — though the text usually stays printed in the code, findable but unenforceable.
feedback.statute: Statutes outrank regulations and common law, but not the constitution. A statute that conflicts with the constitution is invalid.
feedback.common: This is a clash between a statute and the constitution, not a gap for the common law. The higher law — the constitution — controls.
